coredata.dc:title: Preparation of 3D grid structure rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs and its catalytic performance under visible light
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yi
Wu, Mengya
Lei, Weiyan
Shen, Yi
Li, Fengfeng
Peng, Chao
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs composite photocatalyst with a three-dimensional network space structure is designed, to attain high photocatalytic efficiency and adsorption synergy. The synthesized composite is supported by graphene hydrogel which has a surface adsorption effect on the composite materials to achieve high adsorption capacity and rapid adsorption of pollutants. GOQDs promote α-Ag3VO4 photogenerated electron-hole separation and enhance the interface stability and photocatalytic activity of the composite for anti-photocorrosion. The photocatalytic activity of α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs is significantly enhanced with an increasing proportion of rGH. Through the synergistic effect of adsorption and photocatalysis, effective degradation of phenol, tetracycline, ciprofloxacin, and various dyes is carried out. The results showed that the removal efficiency of phenol, tetracycline, ciprofloxacin, and various dyes by rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs (6%) is 96.1%, 98.3%, 99.1%, and 100% in 24, 21, 15, and 3 min, respectively. The trapping reaction measurement shows that the main reactive species for degrading the pollutants are •O2 - and •h+. rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs composite demonstrates excellent photocatalytic and adsorption properties, and efficiently removes a variety of pollutants through the synergistic effect of photocatalysis and adsorption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: α-Ag3VO4
GOQDs
RGH
Photocatalyst
Adsorption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Pollutant removal by adsorption and photocatalysis using rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Science Foundation Project of China
North China University of Science and Technology Graduate Innovation Project

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Science Foundation Project of China (Grant No. 51772099 ， 51572069 ); North China University of Science and Technology Graduate Innovation Project (No. 2019S16 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of α-Ag3VO4, α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs, and rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs. (b) GGA-PBE calculated α-Ag3VO4 charge density graph.
SEM images of (a) α-Ag3VO4, (b) rGH, (c) α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs, and (d) rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs. TEM images of (e) rGH, (f) GOQDs, (g) rAG-6 and HRTEM inset in (h), (i) SAED pattern of rAG-6.
(a) Elemental mapping analyses of rAG-6, (b) EDS spectrum of rAG-6, (c) Physical image of samples.

(a) XPS survey spectra; high-resolution XPS spectra for (b) Ag 3d, (c) C 1 s, (d) O 1 s, (e) V 2p, and (f) valence of rAG-6, (g) FTIR and (h) Raman spectra of α-Ag3VO4, α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs and rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs.
(a) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm of α-Ag3VO4, α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs and rAG-6, (b) TC adsorption capacity of α-Ag3VO4, α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs and rAG-6.

Degradation of (a) phenol, (b) TC, and (c) CIP with the samples, UV–vis spectra of (d) phenol degradation, (e) TC degradation, and (f) CIP degradation with rAG-6. (g) Degradation of RhB, MB, MR, and MO with the investigated samples, (h) UV–vis spectra of RhB degradation with rAG-6, and (i) Reusability tests with rAG-6 using TC.
The kinetics fitting for the photocatalytic degradation of (a) phenol, (b) TC, (c) CIP, and (d) dye.
(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra, (b) plot of (αhν)1/2 vs hν for α-Ag3VO4, α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs and rAG-6.
(a) Photoluminescence spectrum, (b) electrochemical impedance spectra, and (c) EIS results of α-Ag3VO4, α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs and rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs.
(a) XRD patterns before and after the reusability tests and TEM images of the sample (b) before and (c) after the reusability tests.
Effect of various scavengers on TC degradation by rAG-6 (a), ESR spectra of the rAG-6 in the methanol dispersion of DMPO-•O- 2, and (b) the aqueous dispersion of DMPO-•OH.
(a) Mott–Schottky curves obtained for α-Ag3VO4, α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs, and rAG-6 and (b) schematic illustration of electron-hole separation for rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs.
Kinetic parameters for the degradation of phenol, tetracycline, and ciprofloxacin.
Kinetic parameters for the degradation of phenol, tetracycline, and ciprofloxacin.
Recent reports on photocatalytic degradation of Contaminant.

head.ce:title: Preparation of 3D grid structure rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs and its catalytic performance under visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, North China University of Science and Technology, Tangshan 063009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, North China University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, North China University of Science and Technology, Tangshan 063009, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mining Engineering, North China University of Science and Technology, Tangshan 063009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mining Engineering, North China University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mining Engineering, North China University of Science and Technology, Tangshan 063009, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs composite photocatalyst with a three-dimensional network space structure is designed, to attain high photocatalytic efficiency and adsorption synergy. The synthesized composite is supported by graphene hydrogel which has a surface adsorption effect on the composite materials to achieve high adsorption capacity and rapid adsorption of pollutants. GOQDs promote α-Ag3VO4 photogenerated electron-hole separation and enhance the interface stability and photocatalytic activity of the composite for anti-photocorrosion. The photocatalytic activity of α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs is significantly enhanced with an increasing proportion of rGH. Through the synergistic effect of adsorption and photocatalysis, effective degradation of phenol, tetracycline, ciprofloxacin, and various dyes is carried out. The results showed that the removal efficiency of phenol, tetracycline, ciprofloxacin, and various dyes by rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs (6%) is 96.1%, 98.3%, 99.1%, and 100% in 24, 21, 15, and 3 min, respectively. The trapping reaction measurement shows that the main reactive species for degrading the pollutants are •O2 - and •h+. rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs composite demonstrates excellent photocatalytic and adsorption properties, and efficiently removes a variety of pollutants through the synergistic effect of photocatalysis and adsorption.

ce:section.ce:para: With the continuous advancement in industrialization, various pollutants enter the underground water and rivers, causing harm to the ecosystem and human health. Pollutants such as pesticides, fertilizers, antibiotics, medicines, and personal care products are discharged from industries and daily life activities [1,2]. These pollutants have complex composition and high biological toxicity, causing a huge threat to the environment [3]. A catalyst that degrades a certain type of dye or antibiotic is of little significance in practical applications. Therefore, there is an urgent need for catalysts that can degrade multiple types of pollutants.

Semiconductor photocatalysts have broad application prospects because they effectively decompose pollutants into inorganic compounds and convert them into water and carbon dioxide [4]. Konta et al. [5] found that α-Ag3VO4 shows excellent visible light catalytic activity due to its narrow bandgap and has great application potential in photocatalytic degradation of pollutants. However, the inherent chemical properties of Ag3VO4 such as poor light stability [6], high electron-hole recombination rate [7], and photocorrosion during photocatalysis [8] greatly limit its further application in photocatalysis.

Carbon quantum dots [9] (CQDs) are relatively new fluorescent materials with unique optical and electrical properties [10]. After combining with g-C3N4 [11], Ag3PO4 [12], BiVO4 [13,14], Bi2WO6 [15,16], and other materials, they have shown excellent photocatalytic activity and stability under visible light [17]. Graphene oxide quantum dots [18] (GOQDs) are widely used in biomarkers identification [19], fluorescent materials [20], and optoelectronic devices [21] due to their strong electrical conductivity and excellent luminescence properties, but their applications in photocatalysis are relatively small [22]. Reduced graphene hydrogel [23] (rGH) shows strong electrical conductivity, high specific surface area, and low porosity. At present, several studies have been reported on rGH and TiO2 [24], SnO2 [25], NiO [26], BiOBr [27], and Ag3PO4 [28] composite photocatalysts with significantly improved adsorption performance and photocatalytic activities. Dong [29] et al. showed that rGH eliminates the shortcomings of graphene's low bonding interface and easy oxidation while maintaining the π-π stacking interactions [30]. Moreover, rGH has a holistic morphology, which is convenient for its recycling and reuse in practical applications. However, the shadowing effect of rGH decreases its photocatalysis activity [31]. Therefore, we use GOQDs to improve the utilization efficiency of α-Ag3VO4, followed by the loading of rGH to improve the photocatalytic performance and corrosion resistance of α-Ag3VO4.

In this study, α-Ag3VO4 composite with GOQDs is successfully prepared and loaded with rGH. rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs composite demonstrates excellent photocatalytic and adsorption properties under visible light. Our research shows that GOQDs improve the photocatalytic performance and electron transport efficiency of α-Ag3VO4. Loading of rGH not only alleviates the light corrosion but also enhances the adsorption performance of α-Ag3VO4, significantly improving the photocatalytic degradation efficiency. The rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs show high degradation efficiency for phenol, tetracycline, ciprofloxacin, and various dyes via the synergistic effect of adsorption and photocatalysis. In addition, the photocatalytic mechanism is also studied.
All reagents were of analytical grade and purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (China).

GO was prepared by the modified Hummers [32] method. 1 g of natural flake graphite and 1 g of NaNO3 were mixed with concentrated H2SO4 and stirred. Then, 6 g of KMnO4 was added to the reaction mixture and stirring was continued for 2 h at 0 °C. After heating at 40 °C and stirring for 2 h, an appropriate amount of deionized water and H2O2, and the resulting product was GO. Synthesis of GOQDs was carried out by chemical stripping of GO. GOQDs were prepared as a 10 mg/mL dispersion in water [33]. After sonicating GO for 1 h, ascorbic acid (VC) (10 times the mass of GO) was added, shifted the mixture to the reactor, and heated at 60 ℃ for 1 h. The obtained product was washed with distilled water 3–5 times and freeze-dried. The resulting product was cylindrical rGH.

AgNO3 (0.5096 g) was first dissolved in 60 mL deionized water. Similarly, Na3VO4 (0.1361 g) was dissolved in 30 mL deionized water. Then, aqueous ammonia solution and HNO3 solution were added dropwise to the AgNO3 solution under continuous stirring to prepare the silver ammonia solution. Both solutions were mixed and shifted to the reaction vessel for hydrothermal reaction at 60 °C for 1 h. The obtained product (α-Ag3VO4) was alternately washed with deionized water and ethanol 3 times and dried at 60 ℃ for 12 h.

GOQDs (0.0126 g) were mixed with silver ammonia solution and dispersed by sonication for 2 h. According to the synthesis method of α-Ag3VO4, the resulting product was α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs. An appropriate amount of α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs was added to the GO (4 mg/mL) solution. Then, PEG-400 was added dropwise and sonicated for 2 h. Ascorbic acid (VC) (10 times the mass of GO) was added, the reaction mixture was shifted to the reaction vessel and heat at 60 °C for 1 h. The obtained product was washed with distilled water 3–5 times and then freeze-dried to obtain cylindrical rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs. The mass ratios of GO and α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs were 2%, 4%, 6%, 8%, and 10% (denoted as rAG-2, rAG-4, rAG-6, rAG-8, and rAG-10, respectively).

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded with a scanning rate of 3◦ min−1 and 2θ value ranging from 10◦ to 80◦ (D/MAX2500PC, Japan). The morphology and the crystal structure of the nano-composites were characterized by SEM (S-4800, Japan) and TEM (JEM-2010, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 20 Kv, 200 kV respectively. The morphological features of the samples were recorded by energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (Energy dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy，EDS) to determine the chemical composition of composite material. The Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the products were measured using a Fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-700FL, Japan), excitation wavelength is 360 nm. The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of the samples were recorded by a perkinelmer Lambda750s UV–vis spectrophotometer. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Bruker VerteX70 (70 v, KBr) spectrometer. Raman spectra were recorded on a microscopic confocal Raman spectrometer (Thermo Electron DXR) with a780 nm laser light. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were measured using a Thermo Fisher Thermo Scientific K-Alpha+ system with monochromatic Al Kα X-rays. Specific surface areas were measured by a micromeritics ASAP 2460 Surface Area. An electrochemical workstation (CHI 660E) was used to obtain the electrochemical properties with a three-electrode cell including sample (working electrode), Pt wire (counter electrode) and Ag/AgCl (reference electrode). The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed in 0.1 mol/L Na2SO4 solution, the EIS is completed by ZSimpwin software.

The visible light source was a 350 W Xenon lamp (PLS-SXE300/300UV) and a glass optical filter was inserted to cut off short wavelength components (λ < 420 nm) [34]. The reactor was well aerated and stirred with a magnetic stirrer to ensure sufficient mixing. The photocatalytic activity of the prepared samples was tested by the degradation of phenol, Tetracycline (TC) and ciprofloxacin (CIP) and dye (methylene blue, Rhodamine B, methyl red and methyl orange), and concentration as 15 mg/L, 15 mg/L, 15 mg/L and 10 mg/L respectively. Weigh 0.05 g sample and 50 mL degradation product solution into a beaker, the suspension was magnetically stirred for 30 min under dark condition to establish adsorption desorption equilibrium between pollutants and photocatalyst. Photocatalytic reactions were carried in beaker under simulated solar light irradiation using 350 W Xenon lamp fitted with reflector. Centrifuge after irradiation for a certain period of time, and measure the absorbance with perkinelmer Lambda750s UV–Vis spectrophotometer.

The phase composition of α-Ag3VO4, α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs, and rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs is analyze by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) and the XRD patterns are shown in Fig. 1a. The diffraction peaks of Ag3VO4 are in agreement with the JCPDS No.43-0542 [35] standard data file. The diffraction peaks at 2θ = 30.9°, 32.3°, 35.1°, 35.9°, 51.2°, and 54.1° are attributed to the (−121), (121), (301), (132), and (331) planes of monoclinic system α-Ag3VO4, respectively. Because of its small quantity in the composite, the characteristic diffraction peaks of GOQDs disappeared after combining with α-Ag3VO4. The diffraction peak of rGH in rAG-6 also disappeared, indicating the uniform filling of disordered graphene nanosheets in rGH/α-Ag3VO4 to form a three-dimensional network space structure. In rAG-6, the characteristic diffraction peak of Ag (JCPDS No.04-0783) [36] appeared at 2θ = 38.1°, and with the increase in rGH content, the diffraction peak intensity of α-Ag3VO4 decreased, while Ag diffraction peak intensity increased. This indicates that the reducing agent VC slightly reduced Ag+ to Ag [37] during the synthesis of rGH. Furthermore, no additional diffraction peaks and peak shifts are observed in the binary and ternary composites, indicating that rGH and GOQDs did not affect the crystalline phase of α-Ag3VO4.

Fig. 1b is the charge density map of α-Ag3VO4 calculated by GGA-PBE and the charge density distribution around each atom in α-Ag3VO4 is observed. There is a certain overlap between the charge density around the V atom and the O atom, which shows that the interactions between the V atom and the O atom are strong, and the bond energy is relatively high. At the same time, in Fig. 1b, the overlap between the charge density around the Ag atom and the O atom is very small, indicating the weak interactions between the Ag atom and the O atom. This shows that the Ag atom does not lose electrons easily, and the bond energy for the AgO bond in the α-Ag3VO4 system is lower. Therefore, the chemical bond formed between the Ag atom and the O atom in the photocatalytic reaction is easily broken, and Ag+ is reduced to Ag, causing light corrosion.

Fig. 2 shows the morphology and microstructure of α-Ag3VO4, α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs, and rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs obtained by SEM and TEM analysis. Fig. 2a, b, c, and d are the SEM images of α-Ag3VO4, rGH, α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs and rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs, respectively. The petal-like Ag3VO4 particles are observed in Fig. 2a. With the introduction of GOQDs, the size of petal-like Ag3VO4 particles increased and uniformly distributed particles appear (Fig. 2c). The Ag3VO4 particles are wrapped by a small amount of graphene layer, affecting the growth of Ag3VO4 particles. By comparing Fig. 2b and 2d, it can be seen that there are granular α-Ag3VO4 on rGH lamella, indicating the successful loading of α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs on rGH lamella. Fig. 2g and 2h are the HRTEM images of rAG-6 showing the uniformly distributed quantum dots on the surface of rAG-6 and after zooming in, uniform rGH is observed. The ground is loaded with α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs, and the lattice fringe spacing of 0.17 nm can be seen on it, corresponding to the (331) plane of α-Ag3VO4, and the evenly distributed GOQDs in the periphery. Fig. 2i shows the electron diffraction pattern of rAG-6 showing different diffraction rings, indicating its polycrystalline structure. In addition, the size of GOQDs is studied by HRTEM. The average diameter of GOQDs is about 3–5 nm and the specific interlayer spacing distance is 0.21 nm. Fig. 3a and b are the surface scan and EDS spectrum of rAG-6, respectively. Ag, V, C, and O elements are present at their characteristics positions. It can be concluded from these results that rGH is successfully loaded on α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs.

The valence state and binding energy of rAG-6 are determined by XPS. Fig. 4a shows the full spectrum of rAG-6, indicating that the composite material is composed of Ag, V, O, and C elements. Fig. 4b is the Ag 3d spectrum of rAG-6, showing the peaks at 368.3 and 374.3 eV, corresponding to Ag+ in the complex. Peaks at 367.6 and 373.7 eV correspond to the Ag in the complex. These results are consistent with the XRD graphs, discussed in the previous section. Fig. 4c shows three peaks at 284.8, 286.9, and 288.5 eV, attributed to CC, CO, and OCO bonds, respectively, confirming the chemical bond formation between graphene and Ag3VO4. Peaks of composite O 1 s are located at 530.0, 532.0, and 533.0 eV, corresponding to AgO, CN, and CO bonds (Fig. 4d). Fig. 4e reveals that V 2p orbital is in a V+5 chemical state. From Fig. 4f of the valence band spectrum, it is evident that the Fermi edge is located at the midpoint of starting edge of the highest occupied state, indicating that the sample is extremely conductive, consistent with the inherent characteristics of graphene. These results further confirm that rGH and GOQDs in the composite efficiently separate the photogenerated carriers, indicating a strong valence interaction between α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs and rGH.

Fig. 4g and h shows the FTIR and Raman spectra of α-Ag3VO4, α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs, and rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs. Peaks at 730 and 853 cm−1 in FTIR spectrum are attributed to the characteristic absorption peaks of VO4 3-, whereas the absorption peaks at 1388 and 1643 cm−1 are also associated with the stretching vibrations of O-V-O. After combining the α-Ag3VO4 with GOQDs, peaks appeared at 823, 1110, and 1296 cm−1, corresponding to C-H, C-O-C, and C-OH groups of graphene oxide, respectively. In composites, FTIR peaks at 1022 and 1630 cm−1 indicate the stretching vibrations of C-O-C functional groups on rGH, and the peak at 1725 cm−1 belongs to the stretching vibrations of CO groups on rGH. Possibly due to the shading effect of rGH, the absorption peak of VO4 3- shows a redshift. After the formation of ternary composite, stretching vibrations of the graphene skeleton at 550, 1073, and 1383 cm−1 almost disappeared, and the interactions between rGH and binary catalyst changed the characteristic peaks. In Ag3VO4, a Raman peak at 900 cm−1 corresponds to VO4 3- terminal oxygen atom. Moreover, peaks at 813 and 875 cm−1 correspond to the O-V and O-V-O vibrations in the Ag3VO4 framework. α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs composite shows Raman peaks at 1590 and 2714 cm−1, corresponding to the G and D peaks of graphene in GOQDs, respectively. The peak of rGH at 850 cm−1 is attributed to C-H and C-O vibrations in the hydrogel. After the binary material is combined with rGH, the Raman peak of graphene appears between 1335 and 1590 cm−1, corresponding to the D and G bands of carbon.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pollutant removal by adsorption and photocatalysis using rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To illustrate the adsorption capacity of the photocatalyst, N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm test is carried out. Fig. 5a shows that all samples exhibit obvious adsorption jumps, indicating the mesoporous structures of three materials. The specific surface area, pore-volume, and pore diameter of rAG-6 are significantly larger than α-Ag3VO4 and α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs, and the specific surface areas are 2.378, 24.126, and 142.213 m2/g, respectively. This shows that the introduction of rGH and GOQDs increases the specific surface area of α-Ag3VO4, mainly due to the large specific surface area of rGH and strong adsorption capacity of carbon-based materials. The increase in specific surface area increases the adsorption of pollutants, which is beneficial to the formation of the reaction zone, thereby increasing the contact area between free radicals and pollutants and improving the catalytic performance. To further illustrate the adsorption performance of rAG-6, TC is used as a model pollutant. Fig. 5b and Table 1 show that with the introduction of GOQDs and rGH, the adsorption capacities of α-Ag3VO4, α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs, and rGH-6 reached 2.18, 12.12, and 26.47 mg/g, respectively. The adsorption capacity of rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs is 6.83 and 2.18 times higher than that of α-Ag3VO4 and α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs, respectively. With an increasing proportion of rGH, the adsorption capacity increases. However, excessive rGH plays a shielding role for the catalyst, and the photocatalytic performance decreases. This indicates that the addition of rGH greatly improves the adsorption capacity through π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding between α-Ag3VO4 and pollutants.

Figs. 6a, b, c, and g show that α-Ag3VO4, α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs, and rAG-6 efficiently degrade phenol, tetracycline, ciprofloxacin, and dyes under visible light. The removal efficiency of rAG-6 for phenol, TC, and CIP is 96.1%, 98.3%, and 99.1% in 24, 21, and 15 min, respectively ( Figs. 6a-c). The removal rate is 99.1%, and the degradation rate for various dyes is 100%, within 3 min Fig. 6d, e f, and h show the UV–Vis absorption spectra of rAG-6 degradation of phenol, CIP, TC, and RhB, respectively. The results suggest that the addition of GOQDs and rGH not only improves the absorption efficiency of visible light but also increases the charge transfer efficiency of the α-Ag3VO4 and effectively promotes the photogenerated electron-hole pair. Separation efficiency and adsorption performance of the electron-hole pair are further attributed to the excellent conductivity and adsorption performance of graphene. The synergistic effect of the above factors inhibits the photo-corrosion of α-Ag3VO4 and facilitates the visible light activity of α-Ag3VO4. However, as the rGH content increases, the adsorption performance of α-Ag3VO4 increases significantly, but the catalytic performance decreases. This may be due to the shading effect of rGH leading to a decreased transmission efficiency of photogenerated electrons, which affects the absorption of visible light. The photodegradation efficiency of phenol, TC, and CIP by different samples is calculated by the following kinetic equation
ln
C
0
C
t
= kt
. Where the slope k represents the rate constant. The fitting results are shown in Fig. 7 and Table 2, and the optimum loading of rGH is 6%.

To study the optical properties, UV–Vis (DRS) spectra of α-Ag3VO4, α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs, and rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs are shown Fig. 8a. α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs and rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs composites exhibit stronger absorption intensities and a certain degree of redshift in the visible region than pure α-Ag3VO4. This is due to the presence of graphene oxide quantum dots in both composites. After the introduction of rGH, the response to visible light is further enhanced due to a unique up-conversion luminescence effect. Using the Kubelka-Munk [38] formula, the bandgap energies of α-Ag3VO4, α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs, and rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs are calculated as 2.10, 1.80, and 1.71 eV, respectively, indicating that the composite materials easily respond to visible light and have better electronic excitation (Fig. 8b). Fig. 9a, b, and c show the photoluminescence spectra (PL, excitation wavelength λ = 360 nm), photocurrent response spectra, and electrochemistry of α-Ag3VO4, α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs, and rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) shows the separation efficiency of photo-generated carriers in the photocatalyst. As the rGH content increases, the peak shows a certain degree of redshift (Fig. 9a). The luminous peaks for these photocatalysts are observed at525 nm. Compared with pure α-Ag3VO4, lower fluorescence intensity is observed for rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs composite. The peak intensity −6 is lowest for rAG, indicating that the internal electron-hole pair recombination rate is lowest [39], and the response to light is stronger. The quenched fluorescence reveal an efficient separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, which enhanced the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of the rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs composite. As the proportion of rGH increases excessively, the shading effect changes the absorption of light, resulting in a certain degree of redshift. The photocurrent of rAG-6 (6.52 mA/cm2) and α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs (1.42 mA/cm2) is 7.5 times and 1.63 times higher than that of α-Ag3VO4 (0.87 mA/cm2), respectively (Fig. 9b). It shows that graphene has good electrical conductivity, and its conjugated π-bonding [40] effectively promotes the migration of photogenerated electrons and holes and improves the separation efficiency of photogenerated charges [41]. In addition, under long-term light irradiation, the photocurrent of α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs decreases faster than that of ternary composite [42], indicating that the addition of rGH increases the stability of α-Ag3VO4. Fig. 9c shows that the arc radius of the rAG-6 composite electrode is much smaller than that of α-Ag3VO4 and α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs electrodes [43], suggesting that the rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs composite effectively improves the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers. The above optical and electrochemical test results are consistent with the degradation of pollutants, validating that the presence of rGH inhibits the recombination of photo-generated charges, thereby improving the photocatalytic activity.

The stability of the photocatalyst is an important factor in its practical applications. Therefore, rAG-6 is used for repeated cyclic degradation of TC. Fig. 6i shows that the photocatalytic degradation rate after 6 cycles dropped from 98.3% to 83.6%. Fig. 10a, b, and c are the XRD patterns and TEM images before and after the repeated cycle test, respectively. No change in XRD patterns and TEM images is observed after repeated cycles, indicating that the rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs material exhibits good stability during the synergistic adsorption and photocatalysis.

The trapping experiment is used to explore the main active species in rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs for photocatalytic degradation. Fig. 11a shows the trapping experiment by using tert-butanol (TBA), p-benzoquinone (PBQ), and ammonium oxalate (AO) [44] as trapping agents for •OH, •O- 2, and •h+, respectively. Before the addition of the catching agent, the degradation rate is 98.3%. After adding AO(•h+) and PBQ (•O- 2), the photocatalytic degradation rates are 27.4% and 33.9%, respectively. Whereas, the addition of TBA (•OH) has little effect on the photocatalytic degradation rate. The results show that •O- 2 and •h+ are the main active substance for degradation. The ESR test is used to further verify the free radicals generated during the photodegradation process. DMPO spin-trapping adducts are dispersed in methanol and aqueous solution and added to rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs. Fig. 11b shows the ESR measurements. The ESR signals of •O- 2 are detected under light, whereas the ESR signals of OH are not detected under light or dark conditions, confirming that •O- 2 and •h+ are the main active substances for degradation.

Based on the experimental results, we propose a possible degradation mechanism for the rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs catalyst ( Fig. 12b). The Mott-Schottky [45] curve and three-electrode test confirm that the composite material is an n-type semiconductor. Fig. 12a shows that the flat band potentials of Ag3VO4 and rAG-6 are −0.02 and −0.2 eV (vs SCE), respectively. It is well-known that the potential of the conduction band in n-type semiconductors is more negative by 0.1 V than that of flat band potential [46], and the conduction band potentials of Ag3VO4 and rAG-6 are −0.12 and −0.3 eV (vs SCE), respectively, that is, 0.12 and −0.06 eV (vs NHE). Therefore, the potentials of Ag3VO4 CB and VB are about 0.12 eV and 2.22 eV (vs.NHE), respectively, and the energy bandgap of Ag3VO4 is Eg = 2.1 eV. Under visible light, graphene oxide has excellent electrical conductivity and is used as an acceptor for light-excited electrons, and GOQDs, as an up-conversion luminescent material, absorbs more energy than Ag3VO4. Since the size of GOQDs quantum dots is slightly larger than that of Ag3VO4, when Ag3VO4 is excited to generate electron-hole pairs, the photo-generated holes on Ag3VO4 VB are transferred to GOQDs. The direct transmission of Ag3VO4 in CB via graphene oxide ensures the effective separation of photogenerated carriers, thereby reducing the Ag+ reduction rate. At the same time, because the three-dimensional network rGH has a π-conjugated [47] structure and excellent conductivity, it quickly migrates as a channel for photogenerated electrons. The electrons in the ECB of Ag3VO4 are quickly transferred to rGH, which further intensifies the effective separation of photogenerated carriers. e- and h+ are generated in the conduction band and valence band of Ag3VO4, respectively, and the electrons react with O2 to produce •O- 2, which converts the pollutants to CO2 and H2O. After Ag3VO4 loading on the rGH three-dimensional network, the reaction efficiency is further enhanced through the synergistic effect of photocatalysis and adsorption. Table 3.

In summary, we report a composite photocatalyst rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs, supported by α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs through rGH with a three-dimensional network space structure. GOQDs promote the photogenerated electron-hole separation, whereas the directional migration between α-Ag3VO4 and GOQDs significantly inhibit the recombination efficiency of photogenerated carriers, improving the separation efficiency and slowing the photocorrosion of Ag3VO4. In addition, the three-dimensional network structure of rGH enhances the adsorption performance of α- Ag3VO4. When the mass fraction of rGH is 6%, rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs show the best photocatalytic activity and adsorption performance. Under the synergistic effect of photocatalysis and adsorption, it efficiently degrades various pollutants (phenol, tetracycline, ciprofloxacin, and dyes). In addition, the measurement of the trapping reaction shows that the main reactive species in the rGH/α-Ag3VO4/GOQDs photocatalyst are •O- 2 and •h+. This work provides an effective strategy for dealing with the increasingly complex situation of pollutants in actual sewage treatment.

Yi Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – reviewing & editing ，Writing – original draft. Mengya Wu: Data curation. Weiyan Lei: Visualization, Investigation. Yi Shen: Supervision. Fengfeng Li: Software, Validation. Chao Peng: Software, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Science Foundation Project of China (Grant No. 51772099，51572069); North China University of Science and Technology Graduate Innovation Project (No. 2019S16).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2021.162410.
